1:
The prayer of chapter 17 is often called the High Priestly Prayer — a name coined by the Lutheran theologian David Chytraeus in 1561, based on the priestly consecration language of verses 17–19. The chapter concludes the farewell discourse that began at the supper table (John 13), and it is spoken in the disciples' hearing, making it simultaneously an act of prayer and an act of instruction: the disciples overhear the Son speaking to the Father about them. The upward look toward heaven in prayer was standard Jewish posture — it expressed orientation toward God's dwelling place and signaled the transition from address to God's people to address to God himself.

The word "hour" (hōra) has carried theological weight throughout this Gospel (John 2:4; John 7:30; John 8:20; John 12:23, 27; John 13:1). Here its arrival is stated without qualification. The prayer presupposes what the Passover setting makes explicit: the hour of sacrifice has come, and Jesus approaches it in conscious, willing readiness rather than as a victim overtaken by events.
2: The phrasing "authority over all flesh" is a Semitic idiom (כָּל-בָּשָׂר / pāsas sarx) meaning all humanity — the full scope of created human beings. Jewish legal tradition had a precise concept of authorized agency (shaliach): the one sent by another acts with the full authority of the sender and is treated as the sender himself. Jesus's authority over humanity is here framed as a delegated commission — not independently seized power, but authority granted by the Father for a specific purpose. The purpose clause that follows (granting eternal life) defines what that authority is for.
3: The definition of eternal life as knowing God echoes the covenantal knowledge language of the Hebrew Scriptures, where "knowing" Yahweh meant entering into and living within the covenant relationship — not merely intellectual acknowledgment (Jeremiah 31:34; Hosea 6:6; Exodus 33:13). This is the knowing of the covenant partner, not the detached knowledge of an observer. The phrase "the only true God" asserts monotheism in a context where Roman religion was thoroughly polytheist and "gods many and lords many" (1 Corinthians 8:5) were the cultural norm. The appositive "Jesus Christ, whom you have sent" is unusual — this is the only place in the Gospel where Jesus refers to himself in the third person by his full name in prayer, suggesting a formal, almost creedal register.
4: The verb "finished" (eteleiósa) anticipates the cry "It is finished" (tetelestai) from the cross (John 19:30). Both are forms of the same root, teleō. In the priestly context the word is significant: Levitical texts repeatedly describe the completion of temple service and the consecration of priests as "filling the hand" or completing (millē' yad in Hebrew). Jesus frames his entire earthly ministry as a priestly service rendered to the Father — not a task interrupted by death, but a work brought to completion through it.
5: The petition for restoration of pre-incarnate glory grounds the prayer in eternity rather than history: what Jesus asks for is not something new but the recovery of what was laid aside at the Incarnation. The phrase "before the world existed" directly echoes the Prologue (John 1:1–2) and aligns with Second Temple Jewish traditions that described pre-existent Wisdom present with God before creation (Proverbs 8:22–31; Sir John 24:3–9). The audience familiar with these traditions would have recognized the claim as a statement about a figure who occupied the divine side of the Creator–creation distinction.
6: In Jewish thought, God's "name" was far more than a label. The Tetragrammaton (יהוה) was the name above all names, never pronounced in Second Temple Judaism — it was circumlocuted as Adonai (Lord) or HaShem (the Name). To "know the name" of God meant to have access to his character, to stand in relationship with him, to have been admitted to covenant. Jesus's claim to have "revealed your name" to the disciples is a claim to have disclosed the full character of the Father — the same claim the Prologue makes when it says the Son has "made him known" (John 1:18, using exēgēsato, the same word used for authoritative interpretation). The disciples are described as those the Father "gave" Jesus "out of the world" — a Johannine election idiom that appears repeatedly in chapters 6 and 17.
7: The logic of verse 7 traces a chain of revelation: everything given to Jesus comes from the Father; the disciples now know this to be the case. In rabbinic tradition the chain of authoritative transmission was foundational to the legitimacy of teaching: a claim to teach Torah required tracing the tradition back through recognized authorities. Here the chain runs Father → Son → disciples, and the disciples' recognition of this chain is presented as the mark of their belonging to the circle of genuine revelation.
8: The distinction between "giving the words" and "they accepted them" maps onto the honor-shame structure of ancient instruction: a teacher's words were a gift that placed the recipient in a position of obligation. Acceptance of the teacher's words was not merely cognitive agreement but a relational act — acknowledging the authority of the one who spoke. The disciples' belief that the Father "sent" Jesus uses the shaliach concept again: the sender is fully present in and through the one sent, so recognizing the sent one is recognizing the sender.
9: The prayer's explicit limitation ("I am not praying for the world, but for those you have given me") is not a statement of cosmic indifference but a reflection of priestly intercessory structure. The high priest on the Day of Atonement made atonement specifically for the community of Israel, not for all nations — the scope of the intercession was defined by the covenant community. Jesus's prayer here follows this model: he intercedes for those who have been entrusted to him, the nucleus of the new covenant community. The prayer for "the world" in a broader sense comes in verse 21, where the unity of the disciples is itself directed toward the world's belief.
10: The mutual possession of Father and Son ("all I have is yours, and all you have is mine") expresses the covenantal solidarity of the divine relationship using the language of communio bonorum — the total sharing of goods between persons in the most intimate covenant bond. In ancient Mediterranean family structures, a son's assets were in principle the father's and vice versa; the statement intensifies this to absolute mutuality. The claim that glory has come to Jesus "through them" — through the disciples — points toward the community as the site of divine self-disclosure, a theme the Evangelist develops in the unity prayers of verses 20–23.
11: Jesus addresses the Father here as "Holy Father" — a unique form of address in the New Testament, combining the intimacy of the Abba address with the holiness language of Isaiah's throne vision (Isaiah 6:3). In Second Temple Judaism, God's holiness was not merely a moral category but an ontological one: it marked the absolute difference between the divine and the creaturely. Combining this address with "Father" was theologically charged — it said simultaneously that God was radically other and intimately accessible. The protection requested operates through the Father's "name" (character and power), not merely external force.
12: The phrase "the one doomed to destruction" translates a Greek idiom, "the son of perdition" (ho huios tēs apōleias), which uses the Semitic "son of X" construction to denote a person defined by, or destined for, a particular outcome. The same phrase appears in 2 Thessalonians 2:3 for the eschatological adversary figure. Here it characterizes Judas, whose defection is presented not as a failure of Jesus's protective mission but as a fulfillment of Scripture (Psalms 41:9, cited explicitly in John 13:18). The Evangelist consistently frames Judas's betrayal within divine foreknowledge: it happened "that Scripture would be fulfilled," not despite it.
13: "The full measure of my joy" echoes the covenant joy language of the Psalms, where rejoicing in God's presence was the mark of those who dwelt in his courts (Psalms 16:11; John 21:6; John 43:4). Speaking this prayer while still in the world — in the hearing of the disciples — serves a pedagogical function: it models for them what the relationship between the Son and the Father looks like, so that they can inhabit the same register of joyful intimacy. The phrase is not incidental sentiment but a description of the inner quality that the Farewell Discourse has been designed to transfer.
14: The world's hatred of the disciples because they are "not of the world" draws on a distinction that runs throughout the Farewell Discourse. In the honor-shame dynamics of the ancient Mediterranean, a person's community defined their identity, obligations, and social claims. To be "of the world" meant to be shaped by and oriented toward the dominant value system — what the world honors, fears, and pursues. The disciples have accepted a different set of loyalties, which makes them anomalous within that system and therefore threatening. The hostility is not primarily moral but structural: the world rejects those who no longer play by its rules.
15: The prayer's precision here is notable: the request is not for removal from the world's sphere but for protection within it. This directly counters any withdrawal or sectarian reading of the community of disciples: they are not to become a protected enclave removed from contact with the world. The "evil one" (ho ponēros) as a personal figure — an agent of corruption and hostility — parallels the petition in the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:13, "deliver us from the evil one"). The Johannine vision of the disciples in the world but not of it is a mission posture, not a retreat posture.
16: The repetition of verse 14's formula ("they are not of the world, even as I am not of it") creates a structural parallel: the disciples' posture is modeled on Jesus's own. This is not an ontological claim about the disciples' nature but a relational and missional one — their primary loyalty and orientation has been redirected. In a world where a person's community membership defined nearly every social obligation, this reorientation had practical consequences: loyalty to the community of Jesus superseded the demands of ethnic, civic, and familial networks.
17: "Sanctify them by the truth; your word is truth." The verb hagiazō (sanctify, set apart) had specific priestly connotations in the LXX: the consecration of priests and the dedication of sacred objects to God's service. Leviticus 8 describes the seven-day consecration of Aaron and his sons; Exodus 28 specifies the garments and rites by which they were set apart for priestly function. The Deuteronomic tradition also emphasized the Torah as the instrument of covenant formation — the word that shaped the people into God's own community. Here the instrument of sanctification is not ritual action but truth — the body of revelation that Jesus has transmitted from the Father.
18: The sending of the disciples into the world mirrors the language of Jesus's own mission: "as you sent me into the world, I have sent them into the world." The pattern is shaliach extended: the sender is present in the sent one; the original sending is replicated and extended through a chain of authorized representatives. This verse is the Johannine equivalent of what the Synoptics call the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18–20; Mark 16:15), but it is situated within the intercessory prayer rather than as a post-resurrection command — the mission is part of the prayer's logic, not an afterthought.
19: "For them I sanctify myself" — the verb used here for Jesus's self-consecration is the same verb used for the sanctification of the disciples in verse 17 and for priestly consecration throughout the Levitical tradition. A priest did not enter the sanctuary without first purifying and setting himself apart; Jesus presents himself as the priest who consecrates himself before the sacrifice, which is simultaneously the sacrifice itself. The Evangelist's priestly register here is deliberate and developed: Jesus is both priest and offering, and his self-consecration serves the sanctification of those he represents before God.
20: The scope of the prayer expands from the Eleven (or Ten, with Judas now absent) to all future believers — those who will come to faith "through their message." The Greek word for "message" (logos) is the same word used for Jesus's own revelation throughout the Gospel, binding the apostolic witness to the original word of the Son. The Evangelist presents the chain of testimony (Father → Son → disciples → future believers) as the means by which the prayer's petitions extend across time. This expansion is not incidental: it is the theological grounding for the existence of every subsequent generation of the community.
21: The unity prayer ("that all of them may be one, Father, just as you are in me and I am in you") uses the divine relationship as the model and source of the community's unity. In ancient Mediterranean culture, unity within a group was not primarily an emotional state but a practical reality of shared purpose, resources, and loyalty — the internal coherence that made a community effective. The Evangelist presses beyond social unity to something grounded in the Trinitarian relationship itself. The final clause — "so that the world may believe that you have sent me" — makes the community's unity instrumental for mission: it is the evidence that compels the world's recognition.
22: "The glory that you gave me, that they may be one as we are one." The glory (doxa) given to the disciples is not a separate commodity but a participation in the relational reality between Father and Son. In the honor culture of the first century, glory was the public acknowledgment of worth — it had to be recognized and conferred by others. But the glory Jesus speaks of here operates on a different logic: it is given by the Father, passed through the Son, and extended to the community. It creates unity not by increasing individual prestige but by drawing all into the same pattern of mutual indwelling.
23: The phrase "brought to complete unity" translates a perfect passive participle of teleioō — the same root as "finished" in verse 4 and "It is finished" in John 19:30. The completion of Jesus's work and the perfected unity of the community are linguistically linked. The purpose of this unity is explicitly stated: "that the world may know that you sent me and have loved them even as you have loved me." The love of the Father for the disciples is said to be identical in quality to the love of the Father for the Son — a claim without parallel in the Jewish tradition of the period, where the distance between divine and human love was normally emphasized.
24: The desire that the disciples "see my glory" echoes Moses's request in Exodus 33:18 ("Show me your glory"), which Yahweh answered by revealing his character rather than his unmediated presence. The Evangelist has structured the Fourth Gospel around a series of progressive revelations of Jesus's glory (John 2:11; John 11:4, 40; John 12:28; John 17:1), each pointing toward the final glorification. The vision the disciples are being promised is not a visual spectacle but the knowledge that the glory given to Jesus before creation was grounded in love — "because you loved me before the foundation of the world," a love that preceded even the created order.
25: "Righteous Father" is another unique address, pairing the relational term "Father" with the judicial attribute "righteous." The world's failure to know the Father sets up the contrast: Jesus knows him, and the disciples have come to know that Jesus was sent — they stand on the side of knowledge, not ignorance. The address "Righteous Father" implies an appeal to divine justice: the prayer's petitions are grounded not just in relationship but in the righteousness of the Father who will vindicate what has been revealed and protect those who have received it.
26: The final verse closes the prayer with a double promise: continued revelation of the Father's name (character), and the indwelling of the love with which the Father loves the Son. The Evangelist frames the whole chapter as the theological summit of the Gospel's first arc: Jesus's prayer captures in condensed form the entire movement from eternal pre-existence, through the incarnate mission, to the formation of a community that participates in the divine life. What follows — the arrest, trial, and cross — will be the execution of what has here been prayed.
